events.text: Patient is a 56-year-old African American male, height 6'2", weight 248 lbs, BMI 31.8, never smoker, history of obesity and obstructive sleep apnea, strong family history with father and paternal grandfather both diagnosed with prostate cancer before age 60.
At age 47, patient was diagnosed with prostate adenocarcinoma (ICD-O site C61.9, histology 8140/3) after early screening prompted by family history revealed a PSA of 11.2 ng/mL.

At age 47, MRI-fusion targeted prostate biopsy showed Gleason 4+5=9 (Grade Group 5) adenocarcinoma in 9 of 14 cores with perineural invasion; PI-RADS 5 lesion in the right peripheral zone with extraprostatic extension on MRI.
At age 47, staging bone scan and CT were negative for distant metastatic disease; staged as cT3aN0M0 very high-risk localized prostate cancer.

At age 47, patient underwent robot-assisted radical prostatectomy with extended pelvic lymph node dissection; pathologic stage pT3bN1M0 with seminal vesicle invasion and 2 of 24 lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma.

At age 47, prostatectomy specimen showed Gleason 4+5=9 adenocarcinoma with bilateral seminal vesicle invasion, negative surgical margins, and 2 positive right obturator lymph nodes (largest metastatic focus 4 mm).
At age 47, adjuvant androgen deprivation therapy with leuprolide 22.5 mg intramuscular every 3 months was initiated postoperatively given node-positive disease, planned for 24 months.
At age 48, adjuvant radiation therapy was delivered to the prostate bed and pelvic lymph nodes, total dose 66 Gy and 45 Gy respectively, while on ADT.
At age 48, PSA was undetectable at less than 0.01 ng/mL on ADT; treatment was well tolerated.
At age 49, leuprolide was discontinued after completing 24 months of adjuvant ADT; PSA remained undetectable.
At age 51, PSA rose to 0.52 ng/mL with testosterone recovery to 340 ng/dL; biochemical recurrence after prostatectomy, adjuvant radiation, and adjuvant ADT.
At age 51, leuprolide 45 mg intramuscular every 6 months was restarted for biochemical recurrence; continuous ADT was planned given prior node-positive disease.
At age 51, PSA declined to 0.08 ng/mL on restarted ADT; testosterone suppressed to 22 ng/dL.
At age 53, bone scan was negative but PSMA PET/CT revealed PSMA-avid lesions in the T9 and L2 vertebral bodies and left posterior 6th rib; PSA was 3.2 ng/mL with testosterone 16 ng/dL.
At age 53, disease reclassified as metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with new bone metastases on continuous ADT with castrate testosterone level.
At age 53, enzalutamide 160 mg orally daily was added to leuprolide for mCRPC.

At age 53, next-generation sequencing (FoundationOne CDx) on prostatectomy tissue and separate liquid biopsy (FoundationOne Liquid CDx) both identified a pathogenic BRCA2 deletion (exons 12-13 deletion), TP53 R273H hotspot mutation, PTEN homozygous deletion, and MYC amplification; tumor mutational burden 5 mutations/Mb; microsatellite stable; HRD score was 56 (positive).
At age 53, after 3 months on enzalutamide, PSA declined to 0.8 ng/mL; no significant adverse effects.
At age 54, patient developed grade 2 fatigue significantly impacting daily activities and grade 1 gynecomastia while on enzalutamide and leuprolide.
At age 54, restaging PSMA PET/CT showed stable bone disease and no new lesions; PSA was 0.6 ng/mL.
At age 55, PSA rose to 6.4 ng/mL on enzalutamide with new bone lesions on restaging imaging; enzalutamide discontinued.
At age 55, olaparib 300 mg orally twice daily was initiated given confirmed BRCA2 pathogenic deletion; continued leuprolide.
At age 55, patient developed grade 2 anemia (hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL), grade 1 nausea, and grade 1 dysgeusia on olaparib; iron supplementation and antiemetics were provided.
At age 55, after 4 months on olaparib, PSMA PET/CT showed significant decrease in PSMA uptake at all known bone metastases; PSA declined to 1.4 ng/mL.

At age 56, PSA rose to 8.8 ng/mL on olaparib after 12 months of therapy; imaging showed new bone lesions in bilateral femora and a new 1.5 cm lung nodule in the right lower lobe suspicious for visceral metastasis.
At age 56, CT chest confirmed a 1.5 cm right lower lobe pulmonary nodule and a new 0.9 cm left lower lobe nodule; CT-guided biopsy of the right lung nodule was recommended.

At age 56, CT-guided biopsy of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule showed metastatic high-grade adenocarcinoma consistent with prostatic origin, positive for PSA and NKX3.1 immunostains, negative for TTF-1; Gleason pattern could not be applied to metastatic site.
At age 56, olaparib was discontinued and docetaxel 75 mg/m2 intravenously every 3 weeks with prednisone 5 mg twice daily was initiated for progressive mCRPC with visceral (pulmonary) metastases.

At age 56, after cycle 3 of docetaxel, patient developed grade 3 neutropenia (ANC 700/uL) and grade 2 peripheral neuropathy in the feet; pegfilgrastim was added and docetaxel dose reduced to 60 mg/m2.

scenario_blurb: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). Rising PSA on ADT. Treated with enzalutamide, abiraterone, docetaxel, or cabazitaxel. Include BRCA1/2 and homologous recombination deficiency testing. Some with bone-predominant and some with visceral metastases.
